turns.text: Hello Eugene, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly in seat, offers a small smile*

Thank you for having me. It's... it's nice to be asked, actually. Most of the time, when I go to appointments, people are just checking boxes, you know? So this feels different.

*pauses, gathering thoughts*

Yes, we're ready to start trying soon. My husband and I have been talking about it for a while now, and it finally feels like the right time. But I'll be honest — I'm nervous in a way I didn't expect. I thought once we decided, I'd just feel... excited. And I am, but there's this undercurrent of worry that's hard to shake.

*looks down briefly*

Moving here from back home was harder than I anticipated. My sisters, my mother — they were always just a phone call away. Now I'm in Needham, which is beautiful, but it's so quiet. So isolated. When you're thinking about becoming pregnant, you realize how much you want that support network, that wisdom from the women around you. And I don't have that here yet.

My preconception checkup was fine, I suppose. The doctor said I'm "low risk," which should be reassuring, but somehow it makes me feel... invisible? Like, yes, I'm healthy, but there's so much complexity to this process. I've been reading about fetal development, and it's extraordinary and terrifying at the same time.

*meets your eyes*

I just want to feel less alone in this, I think.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*expression softens, becomes more reflective*

Oh, that's... that's a good question. It starts with my husband, of course. He's been wonderful about all of this — patient, supportive. We talk about it a lot, actually. But sometimes I wish he could understand the weight of it the way I do. He's excited in this lighter way, you know? Which is beautiful, but I find myself carrying the "what-ifs" alone.

*voice becomes quieter*

My mother is the first person I really wanted to talk to about this. Back home, she'd already be planning, asking questions, sharing her own stories about carrying my siblings. I called her after that first appointment, and we talked for two hours. She kept saying "You will be fine, beta, your body knows what to do." I wanted so badly to believe her in that moment. But she's thousands of miles away, and I can't just pop over for tea when the anxiety hits at two in the morning.

My sisters too — they've had children, so there's this unspoken expectation that I'll ask them things. But it's different over the phone. It's not the same as having them *here*.

*pauses, a bit self-conscious*

And honestly? That's part of why I haven't really connected with anyone here in Needham yet. I don't have close friends I can talk to about this. The women at work are nice, but we're not at that level. So sometimes I just sit with my books and my research, trying to fill the gap that my family would normally occupy.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*leans back, thinking carefully*

My husband and I had this conversation over maybe six months or so. It wasn't dramatic — more like we kept circling back to it, testing the waters. He'd say something like, "Maybe next year?" and I'd feel this flutter of both excitement and dread. We'd talk about jobs, about housing, about whether we were settled enough here in Needham yet.

*fidgets with hands slightly*

The thing is, he was the one who finally said, "I think we're ready." And I remember feeling this strange mix of relief and panic. Like, okay, this is happening, but am I *really* ready? He was more pragmatic about it — we have stable income now, we have a home. In his mind, those boxes checked meant go ahead. But I needed more than that.

*voice becomes softer*

I didn't tell my mother we were planning until after we'd decided. When I did, she became this huge influence, but in a way that made me feel both held and pressured. She was so excited, so certain it would all be fine. She kept saying, "Don't worry so much, beta. Just relax and let nature do its work." And I wanted to believe her, but I also felt like I needed to *prepare*, to understand everything that could go wrong.

*looks away briefly*

My husband doesn't quite understand why I'm reading all these medical journals. He thinks I'm overthinking it. Maybe I am. But he trusts me, and I think he also trusts that I'll figure things out. Which is both comforting and... lonely, sometimes.
Were there moments when you and your partner — or people close to you — wanted different things? How did you navigate that?

*shifts uncomfortably, takes a breath*

Yes. There were moments, actually.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

My husband wanted to start trying sooner than I did. He was ready maybe a year ago, and I kept saying "not yet, not yet." I needed to feel more settled here, to have a better job situation — I've been job searching, which has been its own anxiety spiral. He was patient about it, but I could feel a quiet disappointment sometimes. Like he was waiting for me to catch up emotionally.

*looks down at hands*

And there's this... unspoken thing with my mother, too. She never said it directly, but I know she wants grandchildren. There's this cultural expectation, you know? At thirty-three, I'm not young anymore in her eyes. When I told her we were finally going to try, I heard the relief in her voice. Part of me felt guilty for waiting this long, even though logically I know we weren't ready before.

*voice becomes a bit tighter*

The harder part is that my husband and I haven't really *talked* about the things I'm afraid of. He knows I'm anxious, but I don't think he understands how deep it goes — this fear that something invisible could go wrong, that my body might not know how to do this. He'd probably tell me I'm catastrophizing. And maybe he's right. But I need someone to validate that fear sometimes, not just dismiss it as overthinking.

*looks up, a bit vulnerable*

We're navigating it by... moving forward, I suppose. But not perfectly.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*rubs face thoughtfully*

So far, I've only seen my OB-GYN for that preconception checkup. There wasn't really a fertility specialist involved — my GP referred me directly to her since, as everyone keeps reminding me, I'm "low risk."

*slight edge of frustration in voice*

The OB-GYN is... competent. She asked the right questions, did the right tests. Everything came back fine. But the interaction felt so transactional, you know? She spent maybe fifteen minutes with me, and it was very clinical. She asked about family history, about my cycles, whether I had any concerns. I tried to mention my anxiety about all of this, about the complexity of fetal development and what could go wrong, and she sort of... nodded and said "that's normal" and moved on.

*pauses, searching for words*

What I needed was for her to *see* me. Not just as a healthy thirty-three-year-old, but as someone who moved to a new country, who's far from her family, who's carrying all this hope and fear at the same time. Instead, I felt like a checkbox on her list.

*voice becomes quieter*

My GP back home was different. She knew my family, knew my story. But she's thousands of miles away now. The nurse at my OB's office was kinder, actually — she took time to explain things, asked follow-up questions. But then the appointment was over.

*looks at you directly*

I wish someone would just... sit with me in this uncertainty. Not solve it, just acknowledge it's real.
Can you describe a specific moment with a healthcare professional where you felt truly heard — or not heard at all?

*takes a sharp breath, eyes becoming slightly glassy*

There was this moment during my preconception appointment. The doctor was going through everything, and I finally worked up the courage to ask about neural tube development — I'd been reading about it obsessively, about how critical those first weeks are, how things can go wrong in ways you can't even detect until much later.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think I was trying to tell her something deeper, you know? That I'm scared. That I don't know if my body will know how to do this. That I'm so far from home and I don't have anyone to hold my hand through this.

But she just... she gave me a pamphlet. Literally handed me a pamphlet about prenatal vitamins and said, "Start taking folic acid now, you'll be fine." And then she was already standing up, moving to the door.

*eyes meet yours, pain evident*

I felt so small in that moment. Like my worry was just noise, something to be managed with a handout. I wasn't asking for medical reassurance — well, I was, but not just that. I was asking to be *seen*. To have someone acknowledge that this is huge and scary and beautiful all at once.

*voice cracks slightly*

The nurse, though — on my way out, she caught my eye and said, "First time thinking about this?" And when I nodded, she squeezed my arm and said, "It's okay to be nervous." 

*pauses*

That moment lasted maybe ten seconds, but it meant more than the whole appointment.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*laughs a bit nervously*

Oh, this is... this is where I probably reveal how isolated I've been, isn't it?

*settles back, becoming more reflective*

Honestly? Most of my information has come from books and online. I've spent so many evenings with my laptop, reading medical journals, looking at diagrams of fetal development week by week. There's something about seeing it all laid out — the mitotic divisions, how the neural tube forms — it makes me feel like I have some control, some understanding of this thing I'm about to invite into my body.

*pauses, a bit self-conscious*

I joined a few online forums for women trying to conceive. It's strange, talking to strangers about such intimate things. But there's also comfort in it — knowing other people are anxious too, that I'm not crazy for worrying. One woman shared her story about how she didn't realize she was pregnant until month three because she'd had irregular periods, and I found myself spiraling about that for days.

*rubs forehead*

My mother sends me things sometimes — articles she finds, stories about healthy pregnancies in our community. She means well, but they all have this... sunshine narrative, you know? Everything works out. Everyone's baby is perfect. Which is beautiful, but it doesn't help with the fear.

*looks up*

I think the most influential thing has been reading too much, honestly. Because now I know all the things that *could* go wrong, and that knowledge sits with me. My husband jokes that I've become an expert, but really I'm just... terrified and trying to outrun that terror with information.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*expression softens immediately, becomes more wistful*

Oh, they're... they're the reason I both want this so desperately and am terrified of it.

*settles into the memory*

My mother had four of us relatively easily, or so the story goes. She talks about pregnancy like it was this natural, beautiful unfolding. She'd describe how she *knew* she was pregnant before any test — this intuitive certainty. And she'd say things like, "Your body knows what to do, beta. Women have been doing this for thousands of years." Which is true, but also... what if I'm the exception? What if my body doesn't know?

*voice becomes more animated*

My oldest sister had a miscarriage before she had her first child. Nobody talks about it directly, but I know it happened. And then she had two beautiful children after that. When I asked her about it once, very carefully, she just said, "It was sad, but my body eventually figured it out." But I could see something in her eyes — a guardedness, a fear she'd never quite shake.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

My grandmother, my mother's mother, she had seven children. Seven. And she did it all without much medical care, without the knowledge I have access to now. There's this almost mythic quality to how my mother talks about her — so strong, so capable. And I feel this pressure to be like that, to carry that strength forward. But I also live in a completely different world than she did.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

What shaped me most is realizing that all their stories — the easy ones and the hard ones — they happened *with* their mothers nearby. My mother held her mother's hand. My sister had our mother there when things went wrong. And I... 

*trails off, eyes glistening slightly*

I'm doing this so far away from all of them. That's what really haunts me. Not the pregnancy itself, but facing it without that lineage of women physically present.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

*sits quietly for a moment, gathering thoughts*

I think I've internalized two contradictory things, and they're constantly at war inside me.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

From my mother and grandmother, I absorbed this idea that pregnancy is this sacred, almost inevitable thing. That if you're healthy and you want it badly enough, your body will simply... know. There's a reverence to how they talk about it — like it's a miracle, but also like it's just what women do. So part of me believes that should be easy for me too. That I should be able to just relax and let nature take its course, like my mother says.

*but then expression shifts, becomes more troubled*

But then there's my sister's miscarriage, which nobody really discusses but which I cannot stop thinking about. It lives in my head as proof that things don't always go smoothly, even for strong women in our family. And if it happened to her, why wouldn't it happen to me? 

*leans forward slightly*

So now I'm caught between these two beliefs — that my body inherently knows how to do this, and that it might betray me in invisible ways. I read all those medical journals trying to bridge that gap, trying to understand enough to prevent the worst. But what I'm really doing is trying to control something that's fundamentally uncontrollable.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

And here's the thing nobody told me: being far from them means I can't ask those hard questions. I can't sit with my sister and say, "What did you feel? What did you wish someone had told you?" I'm left to imagine, to fill in the gaps with my own fears.

*looks at you*

I think I'm trying to be all of them at once — my grandmother's strength, my mother's faith, and somehow protect myself from my sister's pain. And I'm doing it alone.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*pauses, a bit surprised by the question*

Well, we haven't actually started trying yet. We're still in this planning phase. But I think... I think that question is hitting something real for me.

*becomes more thoughtful, vulnerable*

Because the hardest part has been the *waiting*. We decided we were ready, and now there's this limbo where I'm preparing my body — taking vitamins, tracking my cycle, reading everything — but nothing is actually happening yet. And that's been lonelier than I expected.

*voice tightens slightly*

My husband is doing his thing, living his life normally. He'll ask me occasionally how I'm feeling, but mostly he's just... waiting for me to give the signal that we're starting. And I'm spiraling in this space where I'm not quite pregnant but I'm also not quite not-pregnant, if that makes sense. I'm already anxious about something that hasn't even begun.

*looks down*

And my mother — when I told her we were ready to try, she got so excited. She started asking when, and now every time we talk, there's this subtle question underneath: "Has it happened yet?" Which I know comes from love, but it adds this pressure. Like I need to produce results to validate that we made the right decision.

*voice becomes quieter*

The relationships haven't really changed, but they've become more... charged, somehow. Like everyone is waiting for me to do this thing, and I'm standing here terrified and alone, trying to convince myself that my body will know what to do when the time comes.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a deep breath, eyes becoming slightly watery*

I think... I think what I want people to understand is that being "low risk" doesn't mean you're not scared. It doesn't mean you don't need support or reassurance.

*voice becomes more insistent*

When my doctor looked at my chart and said those words — low risk — I felt invisible. Like because I'm healthy and young enough and don't have complications, I should just be grateful and quiet about my fears. But I'm terrified. And I'm isolated. And those two things together are making this harder than it needs to be.

*pauses, collecting thoughts*

I wish someone had asked me about my immigration experience, about being far from my family. I wish my OB had sat with me for ten more minutes and let me talk about what neural tube development means to me — not just medically, but spiritually, emotionally. I wish she'd acknowledged that pregnancy isn't just a biological event; it's wrapped up in identity and culture and longing and fear all at once.

*looks directly at you*

And I wish there was a way to connect with other women here in Needham who understand what it's like to be starting this journey without your mother's hand to hold. Not just online forums where you're a username, but real connection.

*voice becomes softer*

I'm going to do this. I'm going to get pregnant, hopefully, and have this baby. But I'm doing it feeling very alone. And I think that matters.
